IRAN:

U.S. and Iranian forces exchanged heavy missile and drone assaults, with Tehran targeting U.S. facilities in states across the Gulf and saying it had again closed the vital Strait of Hormuz, sending oil prices higher. (RTRS)

The U.S. military said it struck Iranian military air-defence systems, coastal radar sites, missile and drone capabilities, and small boats, using aircraft, naval vessels and drones during operations on Sunday. (RTRS)

The Revolutionary Guards said in a statement on Monday that the only way to restore regular shipping traffic through the strait was to end U.S. military interventions in the waterway, and warned that "continued interference could lead to greater incidents in the global oil and gas sector." (RTRS)

Iran Foreign Ministry said the agreement with the US has “undoubtedly entered a crisis phase,” spokesman Esmail Baghaei says in his weekly press conference. Said as long as the other party violates its commitments, Iran won’t abide by the agreement. SAid contacts to reduce tensions in the region are “continuing”. (BBG)

U.S. officials said around 20 vessels had been escorted through the strait in the previous 24 hours, though ship tracking sites showed little traffic moving. (RTRS)

Six vessels transited the strait on Sunday, ship-tracking data from Kpler showed, the lowest number in five weeks. (RTRS)

MACRO:

Shares of SK Hynix slid more than 15% on Korea Exchange to the lowest level in over two months, while peer Samsung Electronics dropped nearly 11%. The pair drove a 9% plunge in the benchmark Kospi that triggered a brief circuit-breaker suspension for the seventh time this year. The losses came after SK Hynix’s American depositary receipts climbed 13% Friday. (BBG)

Apple sued OpenAI and one of its top executives Friday alleging the AI company stole trade secrets as part of its effort to develop competing devices. OpenAI considered sending a breach of contract notice to Apple this year alleging that the iPhone maker didn't uphold the terms of their 2024 agreement to promote ChatGPT within Siri. (WSJ)

Republican Senator Lindsey Graham, a close ally of US President Trump, has died at the age of 71. He died on Saturday evening following a "brief and sudden illness", according to his office. (BBC)

'The Quarter-Trillion-Dollar Onslaught of AI Bonds Is Testing Investors’ Limits' WSJ article notes the investment-grade corporate bond market has struggled to absorb $75 billion of bond issuance from Nvidia, SpaceX and Amazon in recent weeks, with new issues quickly falling in the secondary market. (WSJ)

Companies from Silicon Valley to Europe are turning to Chinese AI models as they try to cut the cost of using the technology and reduce their dependence on US frontier labs. (FT)

US stocks beyond the technology giants are poised to report strong earnings this season, broadening the equity rally, according to Morgan Stanley strategists. The team led by Michael Wilson said the median S&P 1500 Composite Index constituent is generating earnings-per-share growth of more than 10%, the best performance since the post-Covid recovery. (BBG)

Renewed tensions between the US and Iran shouldn’t deter investors from buying dips as markets revert to a familiar escalation playbook, according to JPMorgan strategists. The team led by Mislav Matejka writes the conflict remains volatile, but believes investors should use the dips driven by geopolitical headlines to add exposure. (BBG)

AI company executives told CNBC they are not seeing signs of overcapacity in the AI buildout. But they suggested that enterprises are looking more closely at how much AI is costing and the return on their investment. (CNBC)

A worldwide AI-driven inflation surge is coming, and the US will likely see the worst of it, Goldman says. The bank estimates that AI will increase core PCE inflation by 50 basis points by the end of the year. (Business Insider)

Japan has no immediate plans to change target asset allocations of ​its state pension funds but could work within existing allowable ranges to direct more investment to domestic assets, ‌according to Reuters sources. The remark sparked gains in the yen and bonds. (RTRS)

A pivot in Japan's massive retirement assets would likely manifest as a slow redirection of maturing debt rather than a fire sale that could roil global markets, analysts say. (RTRS)

Goldman Sachs reiterates its broad preference for mainland‑listed A shares over Hong Kong’s H shares, citing a tactical tilt toward AI‑related hardware technology firms. Goldman’s tactical preference is still with A shares, but it recommends scaling into certain large-cap H-share internet stocks. (BBG)

China instructed some major refiners to keep fuel production high, according to Bloomberg sources, in an effort to protect domestic consumers as strikes in the Persian Gulf once again threaten oil shipments. (BBG)

The 2026 World AI Conference and High-Level Meeting on Global AI Governance will be held in Shanghai from July 17 to 20, China’s Foreign Ministry announced. President Xi Jinping will attend the opening ceremony and deliver a keynote speech. (MOFCOM)

A new, Beijing-backed mining investment vehicle is aimed at bolstering China’s grip on overseas resources, as the country pushes back against US and European efforts to curb its dominance of the mineral supply chain. (BBG)

Demand for Beijing's grade-A office buildings increased in the first half of the year, with artificial intelligence and semiconductor companies making the Zhongguancun area a hot property at a time when office markets nationwide are still struggling. (SCMP)

Europe and the US would need to invest an extra $23.6tn over the next 25 years to end their reliance on China in critical industries such as manufacturing and technology, an economic analysis suggests. (FT)

South Korea expects to reap a record tax windfall from the AI-driven semiconductor boom, giving President Lee Jae Myung’s government fresh fiscal firepower to fund an ambitious investment agenda. (BBG)

The South Korean ruling Democratic Party (DP) and the government agreed Monday to step up support for the government's recently unveiled large-scale chip cluster and artificial intelligence (AI) investment projects while pledging to swiftly push through related legislation. (Yonhap)

The South Korean central bank said the global semiconductor market remains undersupplied and the current AI-driven supercycle is expected to continue for some time, according to a report released Monday, dismissing investors' concerns that the chip cycle has already peaked. (Yonhap)

Asia's imports of liquefied natural gas (LNG) are poised to hit a six-month high in July while Europe's imports plunge to their lowest in nearly two years. The weakness in Europe underscores just how far the second-biggest consuming region is falling behind in building inventories for winter demand. (RTRS)

'Europe's jet fuel stocks are wafer thin as Iran tensions flare' Reuters article notes Britain, France and Germany are particularly vulnerable in a continent where decades of refinery closures left it more reliant than most on Middle Eastern shipments via the Strait of Hormuz. (RTRS)

Europe imported more liquefied natural gas from Russia’s leading LNG project than ever before in the first half of 2026, absorbing nearly all of the Siberian facility’s output months before an EU ban on Russian gas imports comes into effect. (FT)

Growth in the euro zone is set to be weaker this year than previously predicted after hostilities in the Middle East resumed, economists say. (BBG)

Arthur Mensch, CEO of Mistral AI, believes France's relatively cheap energy is a major competitive advantage in the AI race. Mensch argues that Europe possesses abundant affordable energy, but risks it being used by American tech companies, and warns that "in two years it will be too late". (Politico) 

Marine Le Pen extended her lead in a voter preference poll ahead of next April’s French presidential election after appeal judges cleared her to enter the race for a fourth time by shortening a previous ban on her standing. (BBG)

Andy Burnham, the UK’s presumptive next prime minister, is considering unveiling a bolstered budget later this year as allies and experts seek to persuade him to pursue a land tax, public control of utilities and a more ambitious devolution strategy. (BBG)

Deep splits of opinion on the Bank of England’s rate-setting committee are making it harder to rebuild the central bank’s credibility after five years of inflation running above target. Critics — some of them on the nine-member Monetary Policy Committee itself — complain of a fragmented outlook from the bank. (FT)

The UK could benefit from a £33bn economic boost if it accelerates plans to digitise its financial markets, said the chair of a new task force, adding that the country has a “better story than people give it credit for” in tokenising assets. (FT)

The UK stock market is defensive and tilted to commodities, making it less appealing as geopolitical tensions abate, according to Citigroup Inc. strategists. The team led by Beata Manthey double-downgraded the country to underweight from overweight in their global asset allocation, preferring exposure to US and Japanese peers. (BBG)

South Africa plans to increase its strategic oil reserves for the first time since the apartheid government stockpiled crude, adding to measures across the continent to mitigate supply shocks. (BBG)

Foreign investors poured more than $1 billion into Indian equities last week, with Goldman Sachs Group Inc. predicting further inflows as a stable currency and improved earnings expectations prompt funds to add to their holdings. (BBG)

GEOPOLITICAL:

Moscow faced a second day of large-scale Ukrainian drone attacks, with officials reporting hundreds of the unmanned craft flying toward the Russian capital and three fatalities in the surrounding region. (BBG)

Ukrainian agricultural group Kernel Holding said on Monday it had suspended operations at its port infrastructure in Chornomorsk after it was significantly damaged by Russian missile and drone attacks. (RTRS)

Leading banks including Deutsche Bank and UniCredit are suing industrial gases group Linde in a test case over who should bear the cost of western sanctions against Russia, as they seek to recover hundreds of millions of euros in assets seized by Russian courts. (FT)

EQUITIES:

A long-standing conflict between OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and SpaceX and Tesla CEO Elon Musk flared up again on X after Apple filed a lawsuit against OpenAI for alleged trade secret theft. (CNBC)

Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella took a quiet swipe at AI labs like Anthropic for how they train their models. In an X post on Sunday, the Microsoft CEO said that model makers complaining about distillation is hypocritical. (Business Insider)

Meta said on Friday it is discontinuing an AI feature launched this week that allowed users to generate images using public Instagram accounts, after drawing widespread criticism over privacy concerns, including from a Hollywood union. (RTRS)

Mastercard is exploring the sale of a majority stake in Vocalink, the company that runs much of the UK retail payments system back to British banks, as it responds to concerns about a strategically critical asset being under US ownership. (FT)

Nscale's £2bn data centre in Essex has been delayed due to a backlog of grid connection requests, with an electricity supply not expected to be available in time for its planned opening. The company is understood to be seeking alternative ways to power the site and is in discussions with Bloom Energy. (Telegraph)

Real-estate giant Brookfield is in exclusive talks to acquire a stake in Hudson Square Properties in a deal that would value the complex at $3.5 billion and further solidify the status of Manhattan’s West Side as the city’s newest tech and media hub. (WSJ)

TSMC reported quarterly sales rose 36%, meeting high expectations and signaling global demand for AI hardware remains intact. (BBG)

TSMC will add two advanced chip packaging plants in the Chiayi Science Park, the island's science and technology minister said on Sunday. (RTRS)

TSMC plans to hike prices on mature-process chip manufacturing by a single-digit percent in the 4th quarter, media report, citing multiple unnamed chip design houses, with new pricing to take effect in January. (UDN via @dnystedt)

Samsung Electronics said on Monday it aims to bring forward the start of operations of its chip fabrication site in the city of Yongin, south of Seoul, to 2029 from 2030-2031, as it seeks to keep up with surging demand for memory chips used in AI infrastructure. (RTRS)

Fast-fashion retailer Shein has cleared a key hurdle for its long-awaited Hong Kong initial public offering, winning China’s nod for a listing that Bloomberg sources say could see it valued at more than $40 billion. (BBG)

MiniMax Group Inc.’s shares plunged after JPMorgan Chase & Co. slashed the Chinese AI model maker’s price target for the second time in less than a week on value dilution concerns caused by fresh fundraising. (BBG)

Nippon Paint Holdings Co. has made multiple offers for Akzo Nobel NV’s decorative paints business in the past month, according to Bloomberg sources. The Tokyo-listed group made its first offer three weeks ago, with a follow-up offer that valued the business at 7.5 billion euros ($8.6 billion) last week. (BBG)

Helsing said on Monday it had raised $1.8 billion in a Series E funding round that values the Munich-based firm at $18 billion, cementing its position as the continent's best-funded defence technology startup. (RTRS)

Jeep maker Stellantis reported an estimated 10% increase in second-quarter vehicle shipments, driven by new model launches in North America. (WSJ)

Regis Resources said it won’t match a rival US$3.9 billion bid for Vault Minerals made by Genesis Minerals, as a planned merger of the gold-mining companies would then no longer meet its thresholds for value and returns. (WSJ)

Just in: Wall Street earns huge commissions from Space X IPO, big M&A deals.

Driven by the blockbuster listing of SpaceX and the rebound in giant mergers and acquisitions, major Wall Street investment banks will report their highest investment banking commission income in four and a half years this week. The commission income from investment banking business of the five leading U.S.

investment banks, Citigroup and Citigroup, is expected to increase by 27% year-on-year in the second quarter. The total commission scale may reach US$11.1 billion, setting a new high since the industry’s phased peak in 2021. A large part of the commission increase comes from the equity capital markets business.

The total revenue of this sector of the above-mentioned five major investment banks is expected to reach US$2.5 billion. The core driver is the US$500 million in underwriting commissions brought by the SpaceX IPO project to 23 partner investment banks. This is also the highest listing underwriting fee in the world's history.

Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley each received $100 million from the deal.

Brian Mulberry, chief market strategist at Zacks Investment Management, said: "The volume of SpaceX's listing is huge, and we expect that all investment banks participating in the underwriting will benefit significantly." Bankers and analysts have pointed out that a number of large technology companies have completed listings or are about to enter the capital market, including SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic.

However, affected by the high interest rate environment, the listing activities of small and medium-sized enterprises owned by private equity institutions have been relatively deserted.

Commission income from the M&A business of the five major investment banks is expected to surge by about 30% year-on-year, with the total exceeding US$4 billion; this is also the first time since 2021 that commissions in this sector have stood at the US$4 billion mark for three consecutive quarters.

Wall Street investment banks have benefited greatly from the rebound in large-scale mergers and acquisitions worth more than US$10 billion. Morgan Stanley mentioned in a client research report this week that the scale of global disclosed M&A transactions is expected to hit a record high in 2026.

JPMorgan Chase, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Bank of America, Citibank and Both are scheduled to release financial reports on Tuesday, with Morgan Stanley delaying its disclosure to Wednesday. The market expects the combined net profit of these six banks to be approximately US$44 billion in the second quarter, a year-on-year increase of 18%.

The trading departments of various banks will also benefit from the increasingly volatile financial markets and provide customers with transaction matching and financing services. Among them, the growth of stock trading business is particularly eye-catching.

While the investment banking sector achieved growth, losses in the credit business remained at a relatively moderate level, supporting the rise of bank stocks. The banking sector as a whole has outperformed the market in the past two years. This has also led the market to question how much room there is for bank stocks to rise in the future.

Saul Martinez, head of U.S. financial industry equity research at HSBC, said: "The threshold for market expectations has been raised. This quarter's performance should be good, but the market has already predicted this, and the stock price trend has already reflected this." Investors will also scrutinize bank balance sheets for signs of stress on U.S.

residents. At present, in the face of various shocks such as import tariffs, conflicts between the United States and Iran, and rising oil prices, American consumers are relatively resistant to risks. Martinez said: "The real cause for concern is that the conflict escalates again, oil prices rebound sharply, and inflation returns.

Only then will economic growth become worrying."

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 13, 2026

Market Brief

This week, markets focus on the Middle East situation as China releases its second-quarter economic data.

The U.S.-Iran conflict escalates, drawing heightened attention to navigation status in the Strait of Hormuz.
Typhoon Bavi becomes the strongest typhoon to make landfall in China this year, prompting coordinated responses from multiple agencies.

Foreign investors remain bullish on Chinese assets, underscoring the long-term allocation value of A-shares.
Top Chinese mutual fund managers aggressively increase stakes in Hong Kong-listed pharmaceutical stocks.

Top Stories

Global markets face a busy week. Domestically, China will release a suite of key data including second-quarter GDP, investment, consumption, industrial production, foreign trade, and housing prices in 70 major cities; the fuel price adjustment window opens again; and Changxin Technology (https://t.co/U5PpFwxBJO) opens subscription on July 16. Internationally, developments in the U.S.-Iran conflict remain under close watch; Federal Reserve Chair Warsh will testify before Congress; U.S. June CPI and PPI data will be released; and the Bank of Korea is expected to restart interest rate hikes after five years. On the earnings front, A-share mid-year reporting season begins; TSMC (TSM.N) and ASML (ASML.O) will release their second-quarter results; and the five largest Wall Street banks will report earnings. Additionally, the World Artificial Intelligence Conference (WAIC) will be held in Shanghai, and the AGI Summit SF2026 will convene.

Middle East tensions escalated sharply. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps announced the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most critical energy chokepoint. The U.S. military subsequently declared strikes against Iran, and Iran carried out retaliatory strikes against U.S. targets in multiple countries across the Middle East. There are diverging views on the navigational status of the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. Central Command stated that the Strait of Hormuz remains open to all vessels transiting this international waterway in accordance with international law, adding that U.S. forces have completed deployments and are prepared to ensure freedom of navigation is upheld. However, Iran's Persian Gulf Maritime Authority said the Strait is currently impassable. U.S. President Donald Trump, for his part, said that as far as the United States is concerned, the Strait of Hormuz remains open.

Amid slowing global growth and accelerating international capital flows in search of high-quality assets, Chinese assets continue to attract strong interest from foreign institutional investors. Recently, international financial institutions including Standard Chartered (STD.DF), Goldman Sachs (GS.N), Morgan Stanley (1207747042), and UBS (UBS.N) have all reiterated their positive outlook on Chinese equities for the second half of the year. Concurrently, foreign institutions are demonstrating confidence in Chinese assets through intensive research on A-share companies and sustained investment in China’s equity markets.

Giant Power (https://t.co/1zv5TQb20g) issued a clarification notice stating that false rumors have circulated on internet platforms regarding the company’s commercial aerospace business, including claims that it is “the only network-based recovery tether supplier” and that it has signed a contract worth over 2 billion yuan with China Aerospace Science and Technology Corporation. The company has never been interviewed or issued statements on these matters and is not the sole supplier of “network-based recovery” or “aerospace-grade special tethers.” From early 2026 to the date of the announcement, revenue from commercial aerospace accounted for less than 0.30% of total operating income, with negligible impact on performance. The company urges investors to exercise caution and rely solely on official disclosures.

China Macro & Policy

Liu Guozhong, member of the Political Bureau of the CPC Central Committee and Vice Premier of the State Council, emphasized during a research visit in Beijing the need to fully implement measures for flood and storm defense, ensure reservoir safety during the flood season, and safeguard the lives and property of the public.

Typhoon Bavi has become the strongest typhoon to make landfall in China this year. The National Defense Office and the Ministry of Emergency Management, in coordination with the China Meteorological Administration and other agencies, continue to assess the trajectory and rainfall trends of Typhoon Bavi, and have deployed flood and typhoon defense measures in key regions. The National Defense Office activated a Level IV flood emergency response for Shandong on July 12, adjusted emergency responses for Zhejiang, Fujian, Beijing, Tianjin, and Guangxi, and dispatched three working groups to Hebei, Liaoning, and Jilin to assist local authorities.

The Ministry of Water Resources upgraded its flood warning to yellow, indicating that major rivers including Taihu Lake, Jiao River, Qingyi River, Shuiyang River, Chu River, Dongliao River, Liao River, Hun River, Jilin section of Songhua River, and Lalin River may experience numbered floods. Some smaller rivers within the storm zone may face significant flooding, urging the public in affected areas to remain vigilant.

As the impact of Typhoon Bavi continues, the Ministry of Transport has upgraded its heavy rainfall defense response to Level II and its typhoon defense response to Level III. The Ministry has conducted direct coordination with transportation departments in Zhejiang, Fujian, Jiangxi, Anhui, and Liaoning, as well as the Yangtze River Maritime Safety Administration, urging heightened vigilance, inter-agency collaboration, and full implementation of all preventive measures.

The National Development and Reform Commission has urgently allocated 100 million yuan in central budgetary investment to support emergency recovery efforts in Zhejiang following typhoon damage. On July 6, the Commission had already allocated 100 million yuan; it has now added another 100 million yuan to support emergency recovery in Guangxi.

China Equities Markets

International asset managers have recently released updated views on the AI sector, analyzing it from perspectives of profit realization, industry chain vitality, and asset allocation. Multiple institutions argue that while certain segments exhibit elevated valuations and overheated expectations, this AI cycle differs from the 2000 internet bubble in that it is underpinned by genuine profitability and industrial demand. Market opportunities are more structural than broad-based, and investors are advised to focus on profit realization and balanced portfolio construction.

Citic Securities (https://t.co/fLN5a98rLU) released a report stating that after the cooling of speculative trading, attention should shift to the recovery of non-AI sectors. For AI-related segments, preference is given to stocks with strong supply constraints, low valuations, and mid-to-downstream exposure, such as cloud providers, memory, servers, gas turbines, and diesel generator sets. For non-AI segments, select basic metals (copper, tin), new energy (awaiting negative sentiment in the supply chain to dissipate and a return to fundamentals), and chemicals (refrigerants, phosphorus chemicals, polyurethane, dyes, large-scale petrochemicals). The firm continues to favor innovative pharmaceuticals with strong overseas expansion and low-valued securities firms.

Securities Times launched the “Times Economic Eye: Second Quarter 2026 Economist Survey.” As of July 10, 61 responses were collected. Respondents generally maintained a positive outlook on A-share performance, expressing optimism regarding market vitality and the long-term prospects of the AI sector. Regarding whether the AI sector is overvalued, nearly 70% of respondents believe a short-term correction is possible, but the long-term outlook remains favorable.

Net inflows via the Stock Connect program reached 39.055 billion Hong Kong dollars last week, the highest weekly net inflow since April. ZhiPu (https://t.co/QtN5WBGUF7) attracted the largest net inflow at 13.702 billion Hong Kong dollars. Internet giants saw net buying overall, with Alibaba-W (https://t.co/UQPzf8o78D) receiving 8.318 billion Hong Kong dollars in net inflows, while Xiaomi Group (https://t.co/JRmn5zWzZn) saw net outflows of 802 million Hong Kong dollars.

Overview of the new week’s important schedule Warsh attended the Congressional hearing for the first time as Chairman of the Federal Reserve, and the market focused on interest rate signals throughout the week. Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh will attend Congress's semi-annual monetary policy hearings for two consecutive days on Tuesday and Wednesday.

This is the first time that Warsh has gone to Congress as chairman of the Federal Reserve to testify on monetary policy since taking office, and it will also become the most important trading event of the week.

At 22:00 on Tuesday (this article is Beijing time), Warsh will attend a hearing of the House of Representatives Financial Services Committee; on Wednesday at 22:00, he will transfer to the Senate Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs Committee to testify. Both hearings focused on the Fed's semiannual monetary policy report to Congress.

The biggest highlight of this hearing is that the U.S. June CPI released at 20:30 on Tuesday is only 90 minutes away from the first hearing. Warsh can hardly avoid the issues of inflation and interest rate paths.

Markets will focus on how he evaluates the latest inflation data, whether the shock to energy prices is sustainable, and whether he will leave open the possibility of a rate hike in July or September.

The June FOMC statement deleted traditional forward guidance on interest rates, and Warsh later emphasized that the Fed will make judgments based on data and economic prospects. If CPI cools down and Warsh weakens his hawkish stance, U.S.

bond yields and the US dollar may face downward pressure, and technology growth stocks and gold are expected to gain support; if inflation exceeds expectations and Warsh does not rule out further tightening of policy, the market may continue to strengthen interest rate hike trades. In addition, many Fed officials will speak publicly.

At 00:30 on Tuesday, Fed Governor Waller gave a speech. At 01:00 on Wednesday, 2027 FOMC Voting Committee and Chicago Fed President Goolsby participated in the fireside chat; at 20:45, FOMC Permanent Voting Committee and New York Fed President Williams delivered a speech.

At 06:30 on Thursday, Mussallem, the 2028 FOMC voting committee member and President of the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, delivered a speech. At 00:30 on Friday, 2026 FOMC voting committee and Dallas Fed President Logan delivered a speech; at 07:00, Federal Reserve Vice Chairman Jefferson delivered a speech on economic and monetary policy. The U.S.

CPI and PPI in June were released one after another, and inflation and consumption data tested expectations for interest rate hikes. The U.S. June CPI and core CPI will be released at 20:30 on Tuesday. Many institutions predict that the overall U.S.

CPI year-on-year growth rate in June may drop to 3.8%-3.9% from 4.2% in May, and the core CPI year-on-year growth rate is expected to remain around 2.9%. Due to the slight difference in the forecast caliber of institutions, the market is really concerned about the month-on-month data and changes in housing, services, energy and other sub-items.

If core inflation falls significantly, the market may reduce its bets on raising interest rates within the year; conversely, if rising energy prices begin to spread to the prices of goods and services, expectations for further tightening of policy by the Federal Reserve may increase. At 20:30 on Wednesday, the U.S.

June PPI and core PPI will take over, providing investors with the latest clues to upstream price pressures. The U.S. June retail sales released at 20:30 on Thursday will test the impact of high interest rates and rising prices on household consumption.

Whether the data and Warsh's statements corroborate each other will determine the direction of U.S. bond yields, the U.S. dollar, gold and technology growth stocks.

Date: Sunday, July 12, 2026 | Time: Morning Session
Coverage: Global Geopolitical & Financial Outlook
Executive Summary
Global financial markets are entering the new trading week with heightened caution. The dramatic collapse of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire agreement and subsequent military engagements in the Persian Gulf have fundamentally altered the commodity and risk-asset landscape. Markets are now pricing in a sustained "high-risk" premium, shifting focus from pure AI-speculation to defensive hedging and geopolitical energy resilience.
1. Geopolitical & Energy Crisis: Hormuz Remains Stalled
The Strait of Hormuz—the world’s most critical maritime energy artery—remains effectively paralyzed following the military escalation that began on July 9.
Maritime Logistical Impact: Global energy underwriters and shipping firms have largely halted transits through the Strait. Lloyd’s of London has increased war-risk insurance premiums for the Persian Gulf region by an additional 150% over the last 48 hours.
The Energy "Floor": Brent crude closed the week at approximately $78.20/bbl, marking a significant floor for oil prices. Energy analysts at Goldman Sachs and other desks are projecting that Brent could breach the $85/bbl mark early this week if diplomatic channels fail to reopen the waterway by Tuesday.
Global Strategic Reserve Status: With U.S. and European strategic petroleum reserves at multi-year lows, the market lacks a "buffer" to mitigate these physical supply disruptions, leaving energy prices highly sensitive to further regional escalations.
2. Global Equity & Treasury Outlook
The "AI-led" bull market, which defined the first half of 2026, is currently facing its stiffest test as institutional capital rotates into "Hard Asset" security.
Rotation to Defense: Equity strategists report a notable acceleration of capital out of highly levered "growth" technology stocks and into defensive sectors, particularly Aerospace & Defense, Global Utilities, and Food/Ag-Commodity producers.
Flight to Safety: The U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield is expected to open lower (trading at ~4.42% in pre-week indicative pricing) as investors prioritize the liquidity and stability of U.S. sovereign debt over corporate equity risk.
The "Warsh" Fed Context: Market focus remains fixed on how Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh will reconcile his "binary, data-dependent" monetary framework with the sudden supply-side energy shock. If energy prices continue to spike, the Fed may be forced into a more aggressive, inflation-fighting posture, even in the face of a softening labor market.
3. Notable Weekend Headlines
Iran’s Response: Over the weekend, Tehran signaled that it would seek "regional solidarity" to exert economic pressure on the West, raising concerns that the conflict may spread to involve pipeline infrastructure in neighboring Gulf states.
Corporate Tech Resilience: Despite the geopolitical friction, enterprise software remains a bright spot. Companies providing automated cybersecurity and AI-driven logistics optimization tools are seeing an uptick in inquiries, as global enterprises rush to harden their supply chains against digital and physical disruption.
Asian Indices Pre-Open: Futures markets in Tokyo and Seoul indicate a cautious start for Monday. Asian traders are particularly concerned about regional energy import costs, which are already showing up in inflationary forecasts for the third quarter.
4. Key Metrics to Watch (Monday, July 13)
Brent Crude Opening: Whether the price of oil gaps higher upon the reopening of Asian trading desks.
Defense Sector Movement: Tracking the price action of companies like Lockheed Martin, RTX Corp, and Northrop Grumman as they respond to the expanded defense authorization expectations.
Currency Volatility: Movement in the DXY (U.S. Dollar Index), which is acting as a major global anchor during the current period of flight-to-safety.

Brent: surplus spooks the bulls

The Dollar is consolidating ahead of Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra, Portugal, and the release of key US labour market data. In the spring, the rise in oil prices drove the dollar higher, but now the strengthening dollar is putting downward pressure on oil prices. That said, there are already plenty of headwinds for ‘black gold’.

Since the deal with the US was concluded, Iran has exported around 40 million barrels. Coupled with the rise in Russian oil supplies to 4.13M BPD, the highest since the beginning of 2022, this is exerting significant downward pressure on Brent.

Despite flare-ups in the conflict in the Middle East, tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continues. The resumption of operations there allows Goldman Sachs to forecast a 3M BPD surplus in the oil market by 2027. Of this, 1 million BPD will be used to replenish strategic reserves. However, the remaining figure will be sufficient to give crude oil bears confidence.

The only thing likely to unsettle them would be the resumption of large-scale US air strikes against Iran. Donald Trump has not ruled out such action, but favours diplomacy.

According to Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama, the government and the central bank are ready to take appropriate measures regarding currencies at any time as necessary. The official noted that her US counterpart, Scott Bessent, had agreed with this.

If Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra fails to halt the US dollar’s advance, Japan will have no choice but to intervene in the forex market.

Summary: A strong dollar and rising supply are weighing on Brent; Japan is preparing to intervene amid a fall in the yen. (FxPro)

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

$4,000: Just a pause, not the end of Gold's decline

Gold is rebounding at the end of the week after a correction in the odds of two key Fed rate hikes this year. Nevertheless, the price is falling for the fourth consecutive week. Since the second half of the week, bears have been relentlessly trying to push the price below the psychologically significant $4,000 mark but eased their grip on Friday afternoon. At the same time, the long-term moving averages favour sellers, as a ‘death cross’ is looming (when the 50-day moving average falls below the 200-day moving average), reinforced by the fact that the price is trading below this cross. On the weekly charts, attempts to push the price back above the 50-week moving average have failed. However, this is also the price support zone from the end of last year, so we can expect a fairly fierce battle around the current level.

It is also worth noting how the narrative for gold’s price movements has changed. Previously, the escalation between the US and Iran triggered downward price movements, with a clear inverse correlation between oil and gold. But it has now become apparent that both assets can move in the same direction – downwards.

However, we must also consider an alternative scenario, in which, since the end of January, the price of gold has been correcting following three years of growth, reaching the 61.8% level—a classic Fibonacci support level. Under this scenario, we should see consolidation followed by a reversal to the upside, with the first stage being a recovery to peak levels around $5,600, and the second stage a rally to $8,000 as part of an extension to the 161.8% level. We nevertheless view this undoubtedly inspiring scenario as less likely, given the history of previous similar price booms and crashes over the last half-century. (FxPro)

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

Street Signal | U.S. bond yields are linked under the impact of oil prices, and "energy speed bumps" affect the path of global interest rates (Goldman Sachs)

Goldman Sachs' rate trader report revealed an important development in the market last week: U.S. Treasury yields are highly correlated with oil prices. Data show that when Brent crude oil rose from $71/barrel to $79/barrel, the 2-year and 10-year U.S. Treasury yields surged by 12 and 16 basis points respectively.

The transmission mechanism behind this is that rising oil prices have pushed up inflation expectations, which in turn strengthened market expectations that the Federal Reserve will maintain tightening policies, leading to a sell-off in bonds.

The report defines this phenomenon as an "energy speed bump" and incorporates it into the global interest rate forecast model, believing that this will have a spillover effect on other markets, including Canada and the euro zone. One-sentence conclusion: Oil prices have become the main driving force for short-term interest rate fluctuations.

The transmission chain of "energy-inflation-interest rates" is becoming increasingly tight. Investors need to pay close attention to this macro risk factor. Positive/negative: negative for the global bond market, especially long-term bonds. Positive for TIPS and energy stocks.

The market has responded to rising oil prices, but if oil prices remain high, the secondary impact on interest rates has not yet been fully priced in. Catalysts:

1) The next direction of Brent crude oil prices;

2) U.S. CPI data this week, verifying the transmission of oil prices to core inflation;

THE VOL PICTURE

All month, traders paid for protection against a crash and refused to let it go. They kept their hedges through the strikes on Iran, through the Fed Minutes, and even through Thursday's rally when a peace deal looked possible. Then on Friday afternoon they finally sold them. The VIX dropped 0.81 to 15.03, and unlike earlier in the week, this decline came from real selling, not mechanical noise. Even the deep crash protection that stayed bid through every headline this month was let go. Think about that timing for a second.

Then the US struck Iran on Sunday.

The market dropped its guard at exactly the wrong moment. Options are now the cheapest they have been in weeks. Monday is priced for a very quiet day, and even Tuesday's CPI morning is priced cheap, even though the market has been making much bigger moves than that lately. Two things can happen from here. If the weekend strikes turn out to matter, everyone will rush to buy protection at the same time, and that rush will make the move bigger. If the strikes do not matter, the quiet grind simply continues and the cheap options were right. Keep one number in mind either way. A single day where SPX moves 113 points or more, in either direction, changes this whole calm picture.

One more thing, the bigger picture behind the week. Last week alone about 56 billion dollars went into stocks, one of the largest weekly amounts this year, and tech funds are headed for their biggest year of inflows ever. Meanwhile the cash parked in money market funds just hit its highest level ever. That is a huge pile of money waiting to buy every dip, and it is a big part of why the market recovers so fast. But it also means most people are already in. From here, rallies tend to get smaller since there is less new money left to push prices up. And if a bad surprise hits, everyone is sitting on gains with no hedges, so they all rush to sell at the same time. That is how small dips turn into sharp ones. History says a setup like this usually leads to a flat or slightly weaker market over the next two to three months, not a crash.

There is also an early warning sign worth knowing about. Trouble usually shows up in the riskiest stocks first, and it already has. Small Korean tech stocks, the wildest corner of this whole rally, have dropped 36 percent in just eight weeks. The US indexes sit at record highs, but the edges of the market are already cracking, and the big names are always the last to fall.

So keep three simple things on your watch list for the summer.

First, watch the biggest tech stocks. They are the backbone of this rally. As long as they hold their long term uptrends, investors will treat every dip as a buying chance and the market stays supported.

Second, watch oil and bond yields. If both start rising together, it means inflation pressure is building again. That pushes the Fed toward a rate hike, and rate hikes are bad news for stocks. This is the combination that can end the quiet grind.

Third, watch Tuesday's CPI. If the number comes in hot, remember that traders sold their hedges on Friday. A hot print sends all of them rushing to buy that protection back at the same time, and that rush by itself can push the market down hard and fast.

text:
“存储双雄”又暴跌！📉韩国央行竟然这么说

一、核心政策表态：韩国央行明确周期未到顶
韩国央行周一发布报告反驳市场 “芯片周期见顶” 悲观预期，核心观点：
当前全球半导体供需格局依旧供不应求，AI 基建持续拉动存储需求，但高端 HBM 产能扩产受工艺、良率约束，供给释放速度远慢于需求；
本轮周期和传统存储复苏完全不同，底层逻辑是 AI 全面渗透带来产业变革，云厂商持续资本开支具备长期持续性；
HBM 等定制化高端存储生产壁垒高、扩产周期长，行业高景气扩张将维持较长时间；
多家海外大行（大摩、小摩、高盛）一致预判，至少 2027 年前半导体需求保持强劲。

二、盘面反差：利好发声当日，存储龙头大幅杀跌
消息落地当天韩股走弱，存储双雄同步暴跌：
SK 海力士大跌近 8%，三星电子跌近 4%；
近期调整幅度显著：7 月 2 日三星大跌 9%、SK 海力士跌近 15%；即便三星公布二季度近 599 亿美元超预期利润，股价依旧高开低走大跌 7%。

三、股价下跌四大核心矛盾
1. 资金提前博弈远期供给过剩（核心分歧）
短期 HBM 订单锁满、现货紧缺是共识，但市场担忧三星、SK 海力士持续加码先进产线，2027-2028 年集中扩产落地后，存储供需反转；周期板块资金习惯提前计价远期利空，高位估值先行消化。

2. 高位获利盘集中兑现
上半年 HBM 行情驱动存储股大幅上涨，7 月进入机构半年净值结算窗口，海外公募、对冲基金集中止盈减仓，抛压集中释放。

3. 基本面结构性割裂，估值存在隐忧
景气高度集中于 AI 服务器 HBM，手机、PC 等消费端存储需求持续疲软；市场担忧 AI 资本开支依赖举债，行业估值高位、一旦云厂商收缩 capex，存储需求弹性快速回落。

4. 中东地缘风险压制风险偏好
霍尔木兹海峡通行受限，双重冲击韩系存储产业链：
1）韩国 80% 原油、大量 LNG 经海峡进口，能源涨价抬升晶圆厂电力成本；
2）卡塔尔供给全球近 30% 电子级氦气，氦气是光刻、刻蚀必备原料，物流受阻带来特种气体涨价、供应短缺隐忧，压制芯片板块风险偏好。

四、行情核心矛盾总结
          中长期产业逻辑（央行 / 机构）：AI 算力持续扩容，HBM 工艺壁垒高、扩产受限，存储超级周期延续；短期订单饱满、长协锁定业绩，基本面韧性充足。
         短期交易逻辑（二级市场）：股价上半年涨幅巨大，资金提前博弈远期产能过剩 + 半年报止盈离场 + 中东地缘避险，引发估值回调。
         结构性机会：行情分化加剧，仅高端 HBM、存储设备、国产存储材料具备持续逻辑，普通消费类存储承压。

五、后续跟踪关键观察点
HBM4 价格走势、大厂长期供货协议签署情况；
三星、SK 海力士新增产能爬坡进度，供给释放节奏；
云厂商 AI 资本开支指引是否下调；
霍尔木兹海峡通航、氦气等电子特气供应链变化；
国内长鑫科技上市带来存储板块估值重塑。

text:
战争在打，能定价的市场却没开门 —— CME 重开前的最后 3 小时（07-12）

⭐ 一句话：过去三个小时中东连续升级，而 $BTC 一动没动 —— 34 美元。今早报 $64,135，现在 $64,169。

期间发生了什么：伊朗革命卫队正式宣布关闭霍尔木兹海峡、伊朗导弹/无人机打了六个国家的美军基地、科威特一座海上石油钻井平台被炸、美军刚刚打掉霍尔木兹周边的反舰导弹与防空系统。

别把这个「没反应」读成「市场认为无所谓」。真相是：能给这些消息定价的市场（原油、黄金、美股期货）现在根本没开门，而 crypto 是 0.35 倍地量的周末薄盘。定价不会不发生，只是被推迟到 —— 今天 15:00（加州时间）CME 重开，距现在不到 3 小时。

🌍 一、宏观：三天的战争，一分钱都还没定价
美股、原油、黄金全部休市，第二天了。屏幕上挂着的还是周五收盘锚：纳指 26,281.61（+0.29%）｜标普 7,575.39（+0.42%）｜道指 52,637.01（+0.29%）｜费半 12,967.16（+0.06%）｜NVDA $210.96（+4.03%）领涨｜VIX 15.03｜WTI 原油 $71.41｜黄金 $4,113.70。

这一整排数字全部是【战前定价】—— 周五收盘时，海峡还没「关」、六国美军基地还没挨打、科威特油井还没被炸。它们已经过期了。今天 15:00 CME 重开 = 油/金/美股期货一次性补定价三天地缘，这是本周第一个、也是最大的跳空窗口。

🛢️ 二、国际形势：「关了」和「没关」同时是真的
升级是真的：美军一周内第三轮空袭伊朗（约 140 个目标），阿巴斯港、格什姆岛均遭袭；伊朗弹道导弹反击卡塔尔、阿曼、巴林、科威特、约旦、阿联酋六国境内美军目标；伊朗波斯湾海峡管理局称海峡「目前无法通行」，革命卫队正式宣布关闭。

但美国中央司令部、特朗普、联合海上信息中心三方同时否认：海峡是国际水道、南部（阿曼一侧）航道双向通行正常、美军护航就位。

谁在说真话？看船，不看嘴 —— 过去 24 小时仅 11 艘商船通过（8 油轮 + 3 货船），正常日均约 34 艘、峰值 59 艘。独立研究机构 HFI Research 最新观察也对得上：伊朗一侧航道仍有船只，但整体航运十分冷清。

👉 结论：这不是「封锁」，是【通行量砍掉三分之二的争议性半关闭】。差别很大 —— 完全封锁油价是跳空级，打折封锁只是溢价级。

❗ 但今天有两处质变：
1）科威特国家石油公司一座海上钻井平台被无人机炸伤 —— 此前挨打的都是船只和军事目标，这是第一个被直接击中的能源生产设施。供给端的性质从「运输受阻」变成了「产能受损」。
2）美军成体系打击了霍尔木兹周边的反舰导弹阵地、防空系统、革命卫队快艇 —— 问题从「伊朗想不想关海峡」变成了「伊朗还有没有能力关」。方向是拧着的：打击进行中 = 短期利多油价；封锁能力被逐步摧毁 = 中期把溢价往回压。这两股力量会在 15:00 CME 开盘时正面打架，别想当然认定只有一个方向。

⚠️ 还有一处要升级理解：伊朗议员今日明说「今后霍尔木兹海峡的管理将由伊朗方面安排，这是伊朗的【最终】政策」。伊朗要的不是一次临时封锁，是改掉海峡的【管理制度】。如果这成真，油价里要长期含一块结构性溢价，而不是打一波就回落的脉冲。

同时谈判在加速：阿曼「双航道」方案（南航道自由通行 / 北航道需伊朗批准、不收费）；伊阿同意继续技术+政治谈判、卡塔尔参与；伊拉克总理周一访华盛顿签油气协议 + 重建绕开霍尔木兹的输油管（需 2-3 年）。

text:
《2026年北美国别市场观察报告》

2025年美加墨（USMCA）三国贸易总额约1.59万亿美元，贸易节奏深度协同。墨西哥已取代加拿大及中国，成为美国第一大贸易伙伴。美国能源供给与制造业高度依赖邻国，加拿大提供美炼油厂23%的原油摄入量，墨西哥则承接了美国近一半的机动车零配件出口。宏观层面，美国通胀2.7%、失业率4.4%，加拿大通胀2.2%、失业率6.8%。AI基础设施投资驱动明显，罗素3000相关企业营收预期较2023年3月累计增加3000亿美元，AI投资占GDP峰值预期为2%。约49%的美国公司已将IT与软件开发近岸外包至加墨地区，北美近岸远程人才规模快速增长。

政策架构呈现“美国优先制度化”与“加拿大对冲性补贴”的双轨特征。美国通过《芯片与科学法案》（专项527亿美元）、《通胀削减法案》及《大而美法案》构建半导体与清洁能源补贴屏障，且CFIUS审查已扩围至AI、量子及能源等敏感领域，并针对特定对手（含中国）实施严厉准入限制。加拿大则通过战略响应基金（SRF）、战略创新基金（SIF）及加拿大增长基金（CGF）应对关税冲击，提供高比例成本报销，以撬动低碳技术与先进制造投入。美墨定于2026年5月启动USMCA首轮自动审议谈判，关键争议涉及原产地规则及关键矿产供应链安全。

产业布局上，中国企业倾向采用“中国研发部件+墨西哥组装+北美销售”的三角模式，选址向墨西哥北部蒙特雷集聚。信息技术市场预计年复合增长率（CAGR）为7.81%，智能制造市场规模预计2030年达857.2亿美元。金融科技领域预计CAGR为13.9%。中国企业在墨产能布局（如海信产能1000万台）虽享受零关税，但受EAR管制及337调查风险影响。未来USMCA审议可能收紧原产地规则，将直接威胁现有供应链合规性，企业需在谈判前完成原产地链路审计与合规风险对冲。

【关键词】：#USMCA #近岸外包 #原产地规则 #CFIUS #芯片与科学法案 #IRA #大而美法案 #SRF #SIF #CGF #美墨加谈判 #AI投资 #劳动生产率 #罗素3000 #蒙特雷 #海辰储能 #海信墨西哥 #美的北美 #智能制造 #布伦特油价 #EIA预测 #金融科技 #CETA #CPTPP #供应链审计 #合规风险 #EAR管制 #337调查 #近岸外包成本 #北美出海

【观点】：

报告属于典型的信息汇编型研究，数据源涵盖WTO、美国普查局、高盛及德勤等，可信度中等偏上。然而，必须区分“事实引用”与“投行假设”。高盛关于“生成式AI十年内提升美国劳动生产率15%且GDP增量4.5万亿美元”的预测属于情景假设，并非已实现事实，若投资者直接将其作为决策依据，面临极高的模型误差风险。

三个核心变量决定了北美出海的成败：一是USMCA 2026年审议的潜在不确定性，原产地规则收紧将直接击穿中国企业在墨组装的“免税合规”逻辑，该风险是当前最大的单点失效点，报告虽提及但权重过低。二是AI资本支出（Capex）的传导节奏，高盛预判的“峰值—回落”曲线意味着算力基建需求可能面临降速，加墨地区作为外包承接地，将经历从算力配套向软件应用侧的转型，企业需提前布局业务迁移路径。三是美欧关税僵局的外溢效应，美国对欧汽车加征25%关税若落地，势必导致欧洲车企竞相在北美（尤其是墨西哥）构建产能，从而挤占中国企业现有的集群空间与资源，这是报告未充分探讨的二阶影响。

逻辑盲点显著：报告将“近岸外包成本比美国低80%”泛化至加墨市场，这存在严重的统计口径误导。80%的成本优势仅适用于中美洲或加勒比海更低端的离岸人力，墨西哥软开成本实际仅比美国低约35%-50%。此外，报告未提示CFIUS对“穿透审查”的风险，即当中国背景企业在墨工厂的中方持股比例超过美方下游客户的合规阈值时，即便产品合规，依然会因下游客户的主动避险而被剔除出供应链。

抽象层级需提升至“供应链地缘政治”高度：北美出海已从简单的“制造外迁”演变为“合规身份管理”。企业不再仅仅是卖产品，而是在美墨边境通过资本合规与资产所有权穿透，管理一种“身份资产”。决策含义在于：企业必须将“地缘政治条款”写入商业合同，特别是针对原产地规则突变的成本分摊与解约机制。对能源侧而言，EIA预测的布伦特原油均价下行周期，隐含全球油市过剩假设，若OPEC+政策博弈超预期，原油价格波动将直接反噬墨西哥清洁能源项目的投资回报率。企业需在战略规划中引入敏感性测试，而非直接采纳投行基准预测。

AI算力需求将呈指数级爆发；若本次美股上市反馈超预期、投资者需求旺盛，公司不排除后续增发ADR进一步拓宽海外融资渠道，加码全球扩产。

三、美联储半年度货币政策报告（新任主席首份报告），核心要点拆解
美东7月10日美联储官网正式发布提交国会的半年度货币政策报告，这是凯文·沃什上任后首份完整政策报告，下周周二、周三他将赴参众两院接受听证，市场高度关注其鹰鸽立场。
1. 通胀现状：三大因素持续推高物价，AI首次被列为通胀推手
报告明确，今年春季美国通胀再度抬升，5月PCE物价指数仍接近2%目标两倍，三大持续性涨价压力：
美国关税政策滞后传导，消费品成本持续上行；
中东地缘冲突扰动能源供给，原油涨价向下游全产业链传导；
AI数据中心基建热潮，芯片、电力、高端原材料需求激增，直接形成持续性价格压力。
高盛经济学家Megan Peters量化测算：AI带来内存涨价、软件订阅提价、数据中心电价上行三重效应，当前已抬升美国核心PCE同比0.2个百分点，预计2026年底通胀贡献将扩大至0.5个百分点 。
2. 通胀数据分化：存在“温和降温”分项指标
美联储提出差异化通胀观察维度：达拉斯联储截尾平均PCE（剔除极端波动价格）从去年5月2.6%回落至今年5月2.4%，说明通胀内部结构分化，并非全线失控，为政策预留缓冲空间。
3. 经济与就业：AI托底增长，劳动力市场趋于平衡
制造业、工厂产出大幅走强，核心驱动力是全球AI数据中心设备采购潮，美国劳动生产率稳步提升；
就业端6月失业率4.2%，处于历史低位，劳动力供需基本平衡，无大面积失业或用工极度紧缺风险；
海外经济2026上半年增长疲软，中东冲突、美国关税形成外部逆风，但AI跨国投资部分对冲下行压力。
4. 美联储政策底线：承诺动用全部工具稳物价
报告重申双重使命目标（充分就业+价格稳定），明确表态：美联储准备动用全套货币政策工具调控通胀，若联邦基金利率触及下限，将启动非常规政策手段压制物价。当前市场一致定价，年内存在加息可能性。
四、行情深层逻辑总结与后市观察要点
短期行情驱动
SK海力士上市强化HBM存储稀缺叙事，叠加美联储报告确认AI长期资本开支景气，资金回流AI算力、存储、光模块赛道；虽然AI短期推升通胀带来加息预期，但市场更看重产业长期增长空间，科技股走出独立上涨行情。
中期两大核心变量
美联储主席沃什下周国会听证会发言，若释放偏鹰加息信号，会压制科技成长估值；若温和表态，AI行情将延续；
存储产能周期：2027年全球存储新厂集中投产，短期紧缺利好芯片企业，但2027-2028年供给放量或扭转行业盈利周期，需持续跟踪长单定价数据。
产业链机会传导
美股存储大涨将正向传导A股HBM、存储设备、特种气体、光通信板块；商业航天、创新药作为存量资金高低切换赛道，仍具备结构性机会。

text:
7月10日美股复盘：指数收在高位，广度继续扩张，期权结构却退回临界状态

市场正在等待下周的CPI、PPI、零售销售、美联储主席Warsh国会证词以及大型银行财报。周五本身没有足以决定新趋势的宏观数据，因此盘面更像是在期权到期、SK Hynix巨额新股供给和周末风险之间完成仓位整理。非投资建议。

主线：动量稳定，但领导权开始分散

当日的动量交易明显比周四复杂。存储股整体走弱，半导体大致持平，软件、科技硬件、网络与IT设备也相对落后；相较之下，银行、私募股权、信用卡、住宅建筑商、机械、建材、折扣零售和部分服饰股表现较强。Meta上涨约6%，成为大型科技股中最突出的个股，但大型科技整体并没有同步走强。

这意味着市场并未完全回到此前高度集中的AI硬件交易，而是在继续进行广度扩张。资金一方面保留了对AI资本开支的敞口，另一方面开始增加银行、住宅、机械、材料和消费周期股的配置。罗素2000下跌0.49%，说明这种扩张并不是无差别的风险偏好回升，而更偏向有盈利、资产负债表质量较高的周期性公司。

本周动量能够稳定，仍然支持一个基本判断：此前半导体和存储的大跌更多来自拥挤仓位、季节性和技术性去风险，而非AI需求发生根本变化。但周五存储走弱也说明，修复过程不会是单边的，资金需要消化SK Hynix上市带来的巨大新增供给和板块内部的重新配置。

Meta：模型效率成为新催化剂

$Meta 上涨约6%，核心催化剂是市场对Muse Spark 1.1的积极评价。SemiAnalysis认为，该模型在性能和价格之间形成了较强竞争力，推理成本大约只有部分前沿模型的四分之一，并将Meta视为前沿模型竞争中最可信的挑战者之一 。这与本周早些时候的14GW算力扩张、Iris自研芯片和持续加码资本开支，共同构成了一条更完整的逻辑。

过去市场对Meta的担忧主要有两个。第一，公司可能积累了过多算力，资本开支最终需要削减；第二，巨额投入未必能形成可变现的前沿模型。现在这两个问题都得到了一定回应：扎克伯格表示公司需要能够获得的全部算力，而Muse Spark 1.1开始展示较低成本的产品能力。
这不等于资本开支风险已经消失。算力翻倍意味着折旧、能源、网络、数据中心和芯片投入同步上升，短期利润率仍可能承压。周五股价的反应说明，市场暂时更愿意奖励模型进步和商业化潜力，而不是只盯着支出规模。

SK Hynix：供给冲击顺利落地

SK Hynix以149美元定价约1.78亿份ADR，总募资约265亿美元，定价较韩国市场折算价格溢价约3%，成为美国市场历史上规模第二大的股票发行之一。发行获得超过七倍认购，挂牌首日最终收涨，说明市场成功吸收了这笔巨大的股票供给 。

这个结果对存储链的意义不只是“Hynix上市成功”。它证明美国资金对HBM和DRAM龙头仍有很强的直接配置需求，但同时也会改变板块内部的资金流向。此前想获得Hynix敞口的资金主要通过韩国股票、EWY或其他亚洲科技基金实现，如今可以直接买入美元计价的SKHY，机构不再需要承担同样的市场准入和货币转换障碍。

因此， $SKHY 对其他存储资产存在两种相反作用：
正面作用：巨额发行被顺利消化，验证了AI内存的长期需求，也提高了整个HBM产业链在美国市场的可见度。
负面作用：部分机构可能卖出Micron、EWY或其他间接替代品，为SKHY腾出内存板块的配置额度。
中期作用：若SKHY逐渐进入美国半导体、AI和大型成长基金，可能形成持续的被动资金需求。

短期风险：新股定价、稳定价格机制和获配机构的仓位调整，可能让首周价格发现失真。

GraniteShares计划最早于7月13日推出SKHY的两倍做多ETF“SKUU”和两倍做空ETF“SKDD”，但仍以监管生效和正式上市为前提 。如果如期推出，短线资金、每日再平衡和杠杆产品对冲可能进一步放大SKHY的日内波动，因此不宜把首日交易表现直接当作长期定价结果。

存储：利好验证后转入再配置

SK Hynix上市前，市场一度预期整个存储板块都会受益；但周五实际表现是SKHY顺利落地、其他存储股相对偏弱。这恰恰符合“增量资产吸走部分旧仓位”的配置逻辑，而不是简单的行业利空。

Seagate逆势上涨2.3%，主要受益于富国银行将其评级从持有上调至增持。分析师认为近期回调改善了风险收益比，公司存在实现至少50美元每股收益的路径，并拥有较强的资本回报能力。它的走强说明存储链并未遭到全面抛售，市场正在区分HBM、DRAM、NAND和企业级硬盘各自的盈利周期。

对Micron而言，问题也不是基本面突然恶化。公司刚把美国长期投资计划提高至2,500亿美元以上，AI内存需求和产业扩张逻辑仍在。短期压力更多来自SKHY提供了一个估值、增长和产品组合都不同的新选择，机构需要重新确定Micron和Hynix在内存配置中的相对权重。

宽度：金融与周期接棒

银行、信用卡、私募股权、住宅建筑商、机械和建材周五跑赢，说明广度交易仍在继续。下周摩根大通、美国银行、花旗、高盛、富国银行和摩根士丹利将集中发布财报，市场提前交易投行业务、交易收入、贷款增长和资本回报改善的可能性。

银行财报面临的预期并不低。大型银行和区域银行指数今年都曾跑赢标普并创下纪录高位，强劲的投资银行业务和6月约2,000亿美元投资级债券发行也已经被市场部分计价。财报后的重点不是单纯看是否超过一致预期，而是观察：

投资银行和交易收入是否属于一次性高峰。
商业与工业贷款能否延续增长。
净息差是否接近阶段性顶部。
消费者信用质量是否继续稳定。
银行如何看待AI资本开支超级周期。

资金流也支持广度扩张。美国股票基金在截至7月8日的一周重新获得约251亿美元流入，全球股票基金流入约564亿美元，科技行业独占约188亿美元；AAII多空情绪差从前一周的负10.9个百分点改善至负0.9个百分点。资金并未离开科技，只是同时开始寻找科技以外的盈利来源。

宏观：市场进入数据等待期

周五没有重要经济数据，美联储最新货币政策报告也没有改变叙事。报告认为通胀仍然偏高、劳动力市场保持较强韧性，长期通胀预期大致稳定。市场对年内加息的定价升至约34个基点，较一周前增加约6个基点，说明投资者在股市上涨的同时，也在重新接受更长时间的高利率环境。

美债收益率当日上升，曲线呈熊市趋平，前端收益率上行约3个基点。此前3年、10年和30年期国债拍卖均获得良好需求，但强劲拍卖并未完全消除利率压力，因为市场还要面对企业债供给、财政风险、AI资本开支拉动的融资需求，以及美联储更偏鹰的反应函数。

下周CPI将是第一个真正的方向性催化剂。高盛预计6月总体CPI环比下降0.1%，核心CPI环比上涨0.17%，低于市场约0.3%的预期，理由包括新车和二手车价格回落、汽车保险走弱、住房通胀相对温和，以及旅行服务涨幅放缓。如果这一预测兑现，收益率可能回落并重新支持久期较长的成长股；如果核心数据达到或超过0.3%，当前约34个基点的加息定价还可能继续上升。

通胀：AI的双重作用

AI资本开支现在同时出现在多头和空头叙事中。对股票市场而言，Meta扩充算力、Amazon发债、Micron加大投资和SK Hynix巨额上市，都证明AI基础设施需求仍然强劲；但对美联储而言，同样的投资会推高电力、芯片、建筑、数据中心设备和高技能劳动力需求，可能提高中性利率并制造短期通胀压力。

纽约联储主席Williams表示，如果AI热潮持续且需求超过供给，可能形成新的通胀上行风险，甚至推高中性利率；但他的基准判断仍是AI最终会提高生产率，形成较大的正面供给冲击。这意味着AI对利率的影响具有时间错配：短期更像需求冲击，中长期才可能转化为生产率红利。
另一个值得关注的变化，是美联储偏爱的核心PCE指标可能获得约0.2个百分点的同比技术性下调。调整涉及投资组合管理与投资咨询、计算机软件及配件、法律服务等分项的计算方法。这可能在数据层面提供一定缓解，但属于统计方法变化，不能等同于真实需求和价格压力同步下降。

地缘与油价：风险降温但未消失

WTI原油周五下跌0.9%，即使本周美国与伊朗互相发动袭击、霍尔木兹海峡通行受限，油价仍没有延续突破。市场倾向于把当前局势理解为又一次“升级以促成降级”的循环，而不是全面战争重启。

特朗普表示伊朗希望继续谈判，但同时强调停火已经结束；伊朗方面否认下周将举行谈判，并表示如果美国违反谅解备忘录，将准备全面防御。双方表述互相矛盾，说明外交通道可能仍然存在，但协议本身已经非常脆弱。
市场真正关心的不是单次袭击，而是霍尔木兹海峡能否维持稳定通行。全球库存偏低、成品油价格仍高、俄罗斯炼厂又受到袭击，如果海峡长期中断，原油风险溢价可能迅速回归。周五油价没有上涨，只能说明市场暂时不相信冲突会持续升级，不能说明供应风险已经解决。

企业焦点：强基本面不一定带来上涨

Meta上涨6%，展示了高效模型与算力投入结合后可能产生的重新定价。Circle上涨5%，因为获得美国货币监理署最终批准、可以设立全国性信托银行；这提高了其在托管、结算和受监管数字资产基础设施中的战略地位。

WD-40上涨10.7%，三季度收入和每股收益均超预期，公司上调2026财年指引、毛利率同比扩大40个基点，并宣布1亿美元回购。Weyerhaeuser上涨4.2%，主要受益于评级上调，分析师认为其较资产净值的折价接近过去15年均值以下两个标准差。

Delta公布的二季度业绩和三季度每股收益指引均好于预期，并恢复全年指引，但股价仍下跌1.8%。原因是客运收入、单位收入和收益率不够强，市场此前对旅行需求、定价能力和高端客户定位的预期已经很高。这与Samsung利润大增但股价下跌属于同一类现象：财报季真正的风险不是盈利差，而是结果不够超过已经抬高的门槛。

财报门槛：盈利泡沫风险升温

FactSet的标普500二季度每股收益预测，在整个季度内上调了3.4%。作为对比，过去5年、10年和20年的平均变化分别是下调2.0%、2.7%和4.2%。这说明分析师不是像往常一样在财报前降低预期，而是在持续提高门槛。

Samsung的营业利润同比增长约19倍、股价却下跌近7%，是这种高门槛环境的直接例子。接下来的银行、Netflix和大型科技财报，也可能出现“数字很好，但不够好”的反应。尤其是那些已经因AI、资本市场业务或消费韧性大涨的公司，单纯超过一致预期未必足够，指引和未来利润率更重要。

市场目前更接近一个“预期脆弱”而非“经济脆弱”的状态。基本面仍然强劲，但估值、仓位和盈利预测已经把很多好消息提前计价，因此任何边际放缓都可能引发较大波动。

期权结构：净Gamma很高，但稳定体制尚未确认

7月10日收盘后的SPX期权结构出现了一个重要变化。SPX现价7,575.39，净GEX约正81.66亿美元每1%，与周四的正80.63亿美元接近；但Gamma翻转点从7,490上移至7,585，现价反而落在翻转点下方9.6点，约低0.13% 。因此，净Gamma虽然为正，长Gamma压制体制却没有得到确认。i

这与周四有本质区别。周四SPX站在7,490翻转点上方约53.6点，做市商处于明确的逢涨卖、逢跌买状态；周五翻转点大幅上移后，指数没有同步站上新的枢轴。市场并未进入真正的负Gamma环境，但已经从“稳定均值回归”退回“等待方向确认”。

Dealer Action Map仍显示强劲的下方承接。指数上涨1%时，做市商预计卖出约21.53亿美元；指数下跌1%时，预计买入约103.19亿美元，机械性下方承接约为上方卖压的4.8倍。

各到期Gamma也出现明显分化。0DTE GEX由周四的正17.57亿美元转为负5.24亿美元，意味着最短期限不再压制日内波动；但周度GEX大幅升至正39.47亿美元，月度为正13.33亿美元，季度为正8.75亿美元，LEAPS为正25.35亿美元 。中长期持仓仍然提供稳定性，但下一交易日开盘和盘中对消息会更加敏感。

IV30进一步降至12.02%，25-delta偏斜中的put溢价收窄至1.94%。这说明投资者购买下行保护的意愿继续下降，市场总体并不恐慌；但低隐含波动率与负0DTE Gamma组合在一起，也意味着如果下周CPI或银行财报带来意外，当前期权价格对实际波动的补偿可能不足。

U.S. stock futures dipped late Sunday while oil prices rose, but didn’t spike, as investors kept their cool after a weekend packed with new fighting in the Persian Gulf.

Futures tied to the Dow Jones industrial average fell 100 points, or 0.19%. S&P 500 futures were down 0.27%, and Nasdaq futures lost 0.48%.

U.S. oil futures rose 3.2% to $73.70 a barrel, while Brent crude also climbed 3.2% to $78.45. Gold dropped 0.7% to $4,085 per ounce.

Bob McNally, founder and president of Rapidan Energy, told CNN that crude oil markets have been “blowing off this geopolitical risk for years” and described Sunday’s rise in prices as “pretty tame.”

Traders are confident that the worst of the Hormuz conflict is over and see the beginnings of a recovery in ship crossings as well as oil production around the Gulf, he explained, adding that the stock market hasn’t cared about Iran since April.

“So there’s a lot of complacency, a lot of confidence, built into the market right now about oil,” McNally, a former White House energy adviser, said. 

On Sunday evening, U.S. Central Command announced yet another set of strikes on Iran, aimed at “degrading their ability to attack civilian mariners and commercial ships freely transiting the Strait of Hormuz.”

It marked the fifth round of attacks in the past week and the third over the last 24 hours, signaling that the operational tempo is quickening.

The latest wave came after the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps targeted a commercial ship, prompting U.S. forces to intercept an Iranian missile and drone.

Earlier on Sunday, the U.S. conducted a “few strikes” on Iranian missile and air-defense systems as well as small boats around the strait.

Before then, U.S. forces had already hit 300 targets over three prior rounds, with Saturday alone seeing 140 targets bombed, including missile and drone sites, naval capabilities, ammunition storage facilities, communication networks, and coastal surveillance locations, Central Command said.

For its part, Iran has paired its attacks on commercial ships with salvos against its Gulf Arab neighbors, including Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, Jordan and Oman.

Iran has argued that the memorandum of understanding signed with the U.S. last month gives it authority to regulate ship traffic and has attacked ships that are not using a regime-backed corridor that runs along the Iranian coast.

But the U.S. has demanded that freedom of navigation in Hormuz must be fully restored and established an alternate corridor that hugs Oman’s coast. Since early May, U.S. forces have helped more than 800 commercial vessels and 400 million barrels of crude oil transit the strait.

The standoff has fueled increasingly violent skirmishes as Iran seeks to preserve its main source of leverage, namely the ability to effectively shut down Hormuz traffic.

For Sal Mercogliano, a Campbell University professor who specializes in military and maritime history, the recent combat was an ominous sign, as he called the ceasefire a “facade.”

“And it’s been a facade for quite a while,” he said on a YouTube post on Sunday. “And one of the things I fear is that we’re finding ourselves in this undeclared naval war. And an undeclared naval war can escalate.”
